intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: A voice that's self-aware, gently funny, and knows cooking is annoying—uses humor and personality to make the app feel like a friend, not a stern cookbook
facade.label: Dad jokes and winks
facade.acceptImplies: User wants personality and levity in microcopy, UI language, and tone throughout the app—casual, humorous asides are welcome
facade.rejectImplies: User wants a cleaner, more straightforward tone—humor might feel forced or undermine trust in a tool meant to make cooking easier

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking should feel approachable and low-pressure, so soft, rounded UI elements reduce cognitive friction and make the interface feel forgiving rather than demanding.
facade.label: Soft rounded buttons
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward warm, cushioned, forgiving visual language — likely wants the app to feel like a supportive friend, not a stern instructor.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer clarity through contrast and defined edges — precision and scannability matter more than coziness.

evidence.hypothesis: A voice that's self-aware, gently funny, and knows cooking is annoying—uses humor and personality to make the app feel like a friend, not a stern cookbook
evidence.implication: User wants personality and levity in microcopy, UI language, and tone throughout the app—casual, humorous asides are welcome

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, showing a simple, scannable ingredient list (text + minimal numbers) feels less intimidating than a full recipe narrative with steps and technique explanations.
facade.axisTargeted: density of information presentation: dense prose narrative vs. ultra-minimal ingredient-focused text
facade.label: Just the ingredients list
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward stripped-down, ingredient-forward design that removes perceived complexity and intimidation from the cooking process.
facade.rejectImplies: User needs more context, structure, or narrative guidance than just ingredients—they may want step numbers, timing, or reassurance built into the information design.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want zero delay between action and outcome—tapping a button should immediately show what they can make, not load or transition
facade.label: Instant results
facade.acceptImplies: Build for instant feedback loops: tap ingredient → instant recipe matches, swipe → instant next meal, no loading states or animations that delay gratification
facade.rejectImplies: Fluid, leisurely pacing is acceptable—users tolerate and might prefer smooth transitions and staged reveals even if they take 300-500ms longer

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want minimal friction — they prefer single-action shortcuts over multi-step navigation or form-filling. A layout that exposes the fastest path to a recipe (one tap from home) will score higher acceptance than nested menus.
facade.label: One-tap cooking
facade.acceptImplies: Build around command shortcuts, big tap targets, and flat navigation. Minimize steps between 'open app' and 'start cooking'.
facade.rejectImplies: Users value discoverability and choice over speed. They need context (categories, filters, reviews) before committing. Navigation can afford depth.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal information (just do this) vs. detailed explanations (why this works, what you're learning)
axes.poleA: Just tell me or explain why

axes.label: Whether user prefers instant gratification with no waiting vs. enjoying a slower reveal and anticipation of results

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal information (just do this) vs. detailed explanations (why this works, what you're learning)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification with no waiting vs. enjoying a slower reveal and anticipation of results
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, the friction point is decision-making and steps. A single tap to a complete meal (with ingredient delivery or pantry integration) removes both cognitive and physical work.
facade.axisTargeted: Organic (human choice, ritual) vs Digital (automated, frictionless)
facade.label: One-tap dinner
facade.acceptImplies: User values automation and speed over hands-on control. They want the app to *do* cooking thinking for them, not guide them through it.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants some agency or scaffolding in the process. They don't want to feel passive. They may enjoy *choosing* what to cook, just not the execution.

facade.hypothesis: When recipes go wrong or steps are confusing, the app uses upbeat, encouraging language rather than error alerts—turning mistakes into moments of solidarity and humor, reinforcing that even cooking failures are okay
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — reassuring levity vs cold functionality
facade.label: Cheerful failure messages
facade.acceptImplies: The app should embrace a tone that normalizes cooking mishaps and celebrates resilience; error states become character moments rather than dead ends
facade.rejectImplies: The user prefers straightforward, neutral communication without humor in instructional or failure contexts; personality should stay in microcopy only, not in critical moments

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in 15 minutes or less, with ingredient lists you can actually find and zero pretentious cooking jargon.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Today's Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta
12 min
4 ingredients
Sheet Pan Chicken & Veggies
18 min
6 ingredients
Egg Fried Rice
10 min
5 ingredients

What's in Your Kitchen?

Tell us what you have, we'll suggest meals you can actually make right now.

No Time? No Problem.

15-Minute Meals Only
Filter active
